Hierarchical linear model on fish population trends

Model we are attempting to build:

\[N_{i,t} = f(t, \alpha_i) + g(\lambda_i, \beta_i) + \varepsilon_i\]

Where:

  • \(N\) : population size estimate

  • \(i\) : population

  • \(t\) : time step

  • \(f(t, \alpha_i)\) : function estimating the temporal trend in population size

    • \(\alpha\): estimated parameter describing each population’s temporal slope

  • \(g(\lambda, \beta_i)\) : function estimating latent variation in population size

    • \(\lambda\) : latent variable (to capture covariation between populations)
    • \(\beta\) : estimated parameter describing each population’s latent variation

  • \(\varepsilon\): residual variation

Because the model also estimates latent variation, we are accounting for the covariation between populations when estimating \(N\).

This model then contains key information to calculate and extend an index like the Living Planet Index, to show the average change in population size from a baseline value of 1 in the start year.

The first part of the model estimates the temporal trend of each population’s size (\(\alpha_i\)), which contains information about population-level change and community-level change:

\[\alpha_i \sim N(\mu_{\alpha}, \sigma^2_{\alpha}) \]

where: - {} is the average population trend - ^2{} is the variance in this average trend, giving us a measure of how variable the population trends are within the sample.

To calculate the index of change from a baseline of 1 (as in the LPI), the average population trend can then be plugged in to the LPI calculation as follows:

\[ I_t = {\mu_{\alpha}}*t + 1 \]

This trend can then be visualized and communicated with its known (\(\sigma^2_{\alpha}\)).

Setting up

#### import & set up data ####

# from https://github.com/eric-pedersen/groundfish-data-analysis
# note: these are mean biomasses over the whole sampled area with an SE
# not actually raw abundances. But for this purpose, should be ok
load("~/Documents/GitHub/groundfish-data-analysis/data/year_geom_means.Rdata")

time = rownames(Year_Geom_Means_all) %>% as.numeric()

# get number of populations & length of time series
npops <- ncol(Year_Geom_Means_all)
tsl <- nrow(Year_Geom_Means_all)

Building the model with HMSC

# format data
XData <- as.matrix(time)
YData <- as.matrix(Year_Geom_Means_all) %>% apply(2, scale, center = FALSE)
randomEff <- as.factor(1:tsl) # assign a location to pairings of pops

# prepare data
formDat <- as.HMSCdata(X = XData, Y = YData, Random = randomEff, interceptX = TRUE)

# run model
m <- hmsc(formDat,
                family = "gaussian",
                niter = 15000,
                nburn = 5000,
                thin = 5,
                verbose = FALSE)
# save model
saveRDS(m, here("models/hmsc.RDS"))

Model performance

# extract the parameters and format them into an `mcmc` object.
mcmcMeansParamX <- as.mcmc(m, parameters = "meansParamX")

# check for parameter convergence (visually)
traceplot(mcmcMeansParamX)

# Explanatory power 

# (R2 <- Rsquared(m, averageSp = FALSE))
(R2comm <- Rsquared(m, averageSp = TRUE))
## [1] 0.7018875

Predicting the full model

# Predict and plot model over time 

# prepare and format data
formPredDat = as.HMSCdata(X = XData, Random = randomEff)
## [1] "'Random' was included into a data.frame"
## [1] "column names were added to 'X'"
## [1] "row names were added to 'X'"
# predict population size over time from the model
pred <- predict(m,
                newdata = formPredDat,
                type = "response")
# Average N across populations
pred_mean = apply(pred, 1, mean)
pred_sd = apply(pred, 1, sd)

Extract model coefficients: alpha

# extract the parameters and format them into an `mcmc` object.
coefs = coef(m)
temp = as.data.frame(coefs$paramX)
temp$pop = rownames(temp)
temp$pop <- factor(temp$pop, levels = temp$pop[order(temp$x1)])
ggplot(data = temp) +
  geom_point(aes(y = pop, x = x1)) +
  labs(y = "", x = "alpha i") +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0, lwd = .3) +
  coord_cartesian(xlim = c(-1, 1))

ggdensity(temp, x = "x1", add = "mean", rug = TRUE) +
  coord_cartesian(xlim = c(-1, 1)) +
  labs(title = "Distribution of alpha",
       subtitle = paste0("Mean = ", 
                         signif(mean(temp$x1), digits = 3),
                        ", Variance = ", 
                        signif(var(temp$x1), digits = 3)),
       y = "Density", x = "Alpha")
## Warning: `geom_vline()`: Ignoring `mapping` because `xintercept` was provided.
## Warning: `geom_vline()`: Ignoring `data` because `xintercept` was provided.

Extract covariance between populations

# extract all estimated species-to-species associations matrix
assoMat <- corRandomEff(m)

# Average
siteMean <- apply(assoMat[, , , 1], 1:2, mean)

# Plot as heatmap
corrplot::corrplot(siteMean, type = "lower",
                   method = "color", 
                   tl.cex = .4, tl.col = "black")

Extract global population trend

Note: this is just the temporal trend, without the latent variation or the residual error.

Average slope of the temporal trend in population size (LPI-pop)

(lpipop = mean(coefs$paramX[,2]))
## [1] -0.07412419

Variance in the temporal trend across populations (LPI-comm)

(lpicomm = var(coefs$paramX[,2]))
## [1] 0.1161826

So after accounting for the covariation between populations, an average population in this ensemble declined in size by 7.8% every year but the temporal trends vary a lot between populations (\(\sigma^2\) = 0.11, so SD = 0.34).

Compare to LPI

Importing results from the LPI calculated on the same dataset:

rlpi_results <- readRDS("~/Documents/GitHub/hierarchical-lpi/outputs/rlpi_results.rds")
rlpi_results$time <- as.integer(rownames(rlpi_results))
rlpi_results <- rlpi_results[-nrow(rlpi_results),] # remove last value which is negative
hmsclpi = (time-time[1])*lpipop + 1
ggplot() +
  geom_line(data = rlpi_results, aes(x = time, y = LPI_final)) +
  geom_line(aes(y = hmsclpi, x = time), col = "red")
